3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.

- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>

- observable feature: feature value

- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- fur color: black and tan
- fur length: short
- ears: erect, pointed, and slightly folded
- body size: small to medium
</features>
<kb>
- IF [fur color = black and tan OR fur color = brown AND fur length = short AND ears = erect or slightly folded] THEN [21]
- IF [fur color = white and brown/grey AND fur length = long AND eyes = blue or green] THEN [26]
- IF [fur color = white/cream AND fur length = long AND body size = large] THEN [15]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 21 fires because the Query Image shows a dog with black and tan fur, short fur length, and erect, slightly folded ears, which matches the observable features for class 21.
</rule_check>
<response>21</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

<features>
- animal type: cat
- fur color: predominantly white with some light brown/grey markings
- eye color: blue
- fur length: medium to long
- ear shape: pointed, upright
</features>
<kb>
- IF [animal type = dog AND fur color = black and tan OR fur color = solid brown AND ear shape = upright or floppy] THEN [21]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = predominantly white with some light brown/grey markings AND eye color = blue AND fur length = medium to long] THEN [26]
- IF [animal type = dog AND fur color = predominantly white AND fur length = long and fluffy] THEN [15]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 26 fires because the query image shows an animal that is a cat, has predominantly white fur with some light brown/grey markings, blue eyes, and medium to long fur length.
</rule_check>
<response>26</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

raw_response_text:
<features>
- fur color: predominantly white/cream
- fur length: long and fluffy
- animal type: dog
- ear shape: floppy, medium-sized
- snout: medium length
</features>
<kb>
- IF [animal type = dog AND fur color = predominantly white/cream AND fur length = long and fluffy] THEN [15]
- IF [animal type = dog AND fur color = black and tan OR brown AND fur length = short] THEN [21]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = white with brown/grey markings AND fur length = long and fluffy AND eye color = blue/green] THEN [26]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 15 fires because the query image shows a dog with predominantly white/cream, long, and fluffy fur.
</rule_check>
<response>15</response>
